metadata.summary: Agentic RAG embeds agents into retrieval pipeline for multi-hop retrieval, query routing, self-correction, and iterative refinement—trading latency for accuracy on complex queries.

content.title: Agentic RAG (RAG 2.0)
content.summary: Agentic RAG embeds autonomous agents into the retrieval pipeline to enable multi-hop retrieval, query routing, self-correction, and iterative refinement. Unlike traditional single-shot RAG, agents plan decompositions, grade retrieved documents, rewrite failed queries, and verify answers before returning — trading latency for higher accuracy on complex queries.

why.p: Single-shot RAG fails on multi-hop questions and mixed-source corpora because one retrieval pass cannot cover heterogeneous knowledge or detect its own gaps. Agentic patterns (Corrective RAG, Adaptive RAG, multi-agent orchestration) add a feedback loop: retrieve → grade → rewrite → re-retrieve, capped at 2-3 retries before fallback to web search. Cap retries at 2; beyond that, precision drops faster than it rises.

list.item: Queries require multi-hop reasoning across documents
Single-shot retrieval consistently fails for complex or ambiguous questions
Knowledge base spans multiple heterogeneous sources (internal docs, web, APIs)
Self-correction is essential: domain where one retrieval miss produces costly wrong answers (legal, medical, financial)
Complex pipelines already have observability tooling (traces, per-turn cost logging)
Simple factual Q&A with a single knowledge base and high recall — standard RAG is faster and cheaper
Latency budget under 2 seconds — agentic loops add 2-10x more turns than static RAG
The orchestration infrastructure does not exist yet — build standard RAG first, then layer agentic behavior
Cost constraints are tight — multi-hop retrieval multiplies token usage; each agent turn = one LLM call
No observability tooling — debugging agentic loop failures without traces is impractical

rule.statement: Select the architecture pattern before building: Single-Agent Router for well-defined sources, Corrective RAG (CRAG) for self-correcting accuracy, Adaptive RAG for query-complexity routing, Multi-Agent for parallel specialized retrieval.
rule.rationale: Each pattern has a distinct latency/cost/accuracy trade-off. Mixing patterns without deliberate design produces unpredictable loops.

example.title: Corrective RAG loop
example.note: Retrieve → grade → rewrite (max 2x) → fallback to web search → generate → verify

example.title: Adaptive RAG router
example.note: Classifier routes by complexity — simple → direct, moderate → single-hop, complex → multi-hop

rule.statement: Implement all four control components: Query Router, Document Grader, Query Rewriter, Response Verifier. Skipping any one causes silent quality degradation.
rule.rationale: Without grading, irrelevant documents contaminate context. Without rewriting, failed queries loop forever or fall back immediately. Without verification, hallucinations pass undetected.

rule.statement: Use a cheap model (Haiku/gpt-4o-mini) for grading and rewriting; reserve Sonnet/Opus only for final synthesis.
rule.rationale: Grading and rewriting are classification tasks, not generation tasks. Using frontier models for them multiplies cost per query 3-5x with minimal quality gain.

example.title: Query router with structured output
example.note: Routes to vectorstore, web_search, direct_response, or api

rule.statement: In multi-agent RAG, give each agent a strict input/output schema and validate at every boundary — message schema drift is the primary source of silent failures.
rule.rationale: Agents passing loosely structured text to each other accumulate interpretation errors across hops. A typed contract at each boundary catches mismatches early. For multi-agent architectures, give each agent a strict contract (input schema + output schema) and validate at the boundary — message schema drift is the #1 source of silent multi-agent failures.

example.title: Unvalidated agent output
example.note: Passing unstructured agent output to the next agent without validation
example.code: # BAD: agent output passed as raw string result = agent_a.run(query) final = agent_b.run(result) # No schema validation — silent corruption

example.title: Validated agent output
example.note: Validate agent output schema before passing downstream

rule.statement: Use these default thresholds: relevance grade >= 0.7 → include document; max rewrite attempts = 2; max retrieval iterations = 3-5; verification score >= 0.8 → output; below threshold → regenerate or caveat.
rule.rationale: Beyond 2 rewrites, query drift from original intent exceeds any retrieval gain. Beyond 5 iterations, token cost grows without bound.

rule.statement: Implement parallel retrieval for independent sub-queries — fan out, collect all results, then aggregate. Reduces total latency by 50-70%.
rule.rationale: Sequential sub-query retrieval accumulates round-trip latencies. Independent sub-queries have no data dependency so parallelization is safe. Execute independent tool calls in parallel (use asyncio.gather with async tool implementations) to reduce latency in multi-step pipelines.

rule.statement: Set a hard token budget per agentic loop (e.g., 50K tokens). If cumulative tokens exceed the cap, abort and return a partial answer rather than running indefinitely.
rule.rationale: Without a cap, a stuck rewriter loop can generate thousands of tokens per query at unbounded cost.

antipattern.description: Infinite rewrite loop: poorly graded relevance causes the agent to retry indefinitely without converging.
antipattern.reason: Each rewrite degrades from original intent; after 3+ rewrites, the agent may search for something unrelated to the user's question. Infinite loops: a poorly graded relevance check can cause the agent to retry indefinitely without converging.

antipattern.description: Grader LLM hallucination: the relevance grader marks a document relevant because it shares topic vocabulary, not because it answers the query.
antipattern.reason: LLM-based graders have the same hallucination risks as generation models. A document about "Python async" may score relevant for "Python async error" even if it contains no error information. Relevance grader hallucination: the grader LLM may incorrectly mark relevant documents as irrelevant, silently dropping good context.

antipattern.description: State corruption in multi-agent coordination: if one agent crashes mid-loop and state is not persisted, the orchestrator restarts with partial context.
antipattern.reason: In-memory state is lost on crash. Production pipelines must checkpoint agent state to durable storage (PostgreSQL, Redis) between hops.

antipattern.description: Response verifier treated as a guarantee: verifier LLM itself can fail to detect hallucinations if the hallucinated content is plausibly worded.
antipattern.reason: Treat verifier output as a probabilistic signal, not a fact-check. For high-stakes domains, add a human review checkpoint before returning the final answer. Response verifier is itself an LLM: it can fail to detect hallucinations if the hallucinated content is plausibly worded — treat verifier output as a probabilistic signal, not a guarantee.

rule.statement: Log every agent turn with: query, retrieved chunks, grading decision, and rewrite reason. This is mandatory for debugging where the pipeline diverges.
rule.rationale: Without per-turn logs, diagnosing a bad final answer requires reconstructing the full pipeline state from scratch. Log every agent turn with: query, retrieved chunks, grading decision, and rewrite — essential for debugging where the pipeline diverges.

rule.statement: For high-stakes domains (legal, medical), insert a human-in-loop checkpoint before the final synthesized answer is returned — surface top-3 candidate answers for expert selection.
rule.rationale: No automated pipeline achieves 100% accuracy. Expert review of candidates is cheaper than the cost of acting on a wrong answer. Human-in-loop checkpoint: for high-stakes domains (legal, medical), insert a human review step before the final synthesized answer is returned — the agent can surface top-3 candidate answers for expert selection.

section.name: Agentic Workflow and Recommended Subagents
section.note: An orchestrator agent receives the query and runs an adaptive decision: simple queries go straight to a single retrieval call; complex queries trigger decomposition into sub-queries. Each sub-query is sent to a retrieval subagent that fetches candidates, grades relevance, and returns {chunks, relevance_scores}. If a sub-query returns no relevant chunks, the query rewriter rewrites and retries (max 2 times). The orchestrator synthesizes all sub-answers into a final response, then the response verifier validates factual grounding before returning to the user. A subagent implementing tool use should define a tool registry (name → callable), convert the registry to provider-specific schema, run the request-execute-respond loop with an iteration cap, and return a structured summary of tools called and their results. For autonomous agents, the subagent should log every tool call (name, args, result, latency) to a persistent trace so errors can be replayed.

Recommended subagents: retrieval-grader (receives a query + document, outputs {relevant: bool, score: float, reason: str}), query-rewriter (receives a failed query + previous results, outputs a reformulated query string), response-verifier (checks that every factual claim in the final answer cites at least one retrieved chunk).

section.name: CLI Tools and Frameworks
section.text: llama-index — AgentRunner, RouterQueryEngine, SubQuestionQueryEngine (pip install llama-index / https://docs.llamaindex.ai/) langchain — CRAG chains, LangGraph for stateful agentic RAG (pip install langchain langgraph) langgraph — Graph-based agent state machines (pip install langgraph / https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/) anthropic — Claude API for agent reasoning steps (pip install anthropic)

section.name: Services and Platforms
section.text: LlamaIndex AgentRunner (OSS, Yes — Python) — Built-in ReAct + tool-use loop for RAG LangGraph (OSS, Yes — Python) — State machine for CRAG/Adaptive RAG workflows Weaviate (SaaS + OSS, Yes) — Native hybrid search; multi-tenant; GraphQL API Qdrant (SaaS + OSS, Yes) — Fast; filterable; supports named collections per agent Cohere Rerank (SaaS, Yes — REST) — Reranking step in the grading phase Tavily (SaaS, Yes — REST) — Web search tool for fallback retrieval Langfuse (SaaS + OSS, Yes) — Trace multi-hop agent loops; see per-turn costs

list.item: Cap retries at 2 per sub-query — beyond 2 rewrites, precision drops; fall back to web search instead
Use a cheap model (Haiku) for relevance grading and query rewriting; reserve Sonnet/Opus for final synthesis
Implement parallel retrieval for independent sub-queries — fan out, wait for all results, then aggregate
Log every agent turn with: query, retrieved chunks, grading decision, and rewrite — essential for debugging
Set a hard token budget per agentic loop: if cumulative tokens exceed 50K, abort and return a partial answer
For multi-agent architectures, give each agent a strict contract (input schema + output schema) and validate at the boundary

see-also.link: NVIDIA: Traditional RAG vs Agentic RAG

template.purpose: TypedDict / dataclass state schemas for CRAG, Adaptive RAG, multi-agent RAG
Pydantic models: RouteQuery, GradeDocument, RewriteQuery, SufficiencyCheck, VerificationResult
